mostImportant: What it holds — below-investment-grade ("junk") corporate bonds selected by a rules-based index, often via sampling rather than full replication.
The character of the portfolio — credit-driven, so its return and risk come from default and spread risk far more than from interest rates.
The nature of its income — a high taxable yield that compensates for real default risk, with equity-like drawdowns in credit stress.
greenFlags: Captures fallen angels (downgraded IG) — they historically outperform original-issue HY because the market over-sells the downgrade.
Publishes the CCC % and sector breakdown plainly (HYG/JNK/USHY) — you can see the real risk being taken.
Spread over comparable IG in line with peers — the yield is paid for with measured, disclosed credit risk.
redFlags: Single-sector concentration above ~25% (often energy) — a sector bet hiding inside a 'diversified HY' label.
Heavy sampling (~600 of ~2,500 bonds) with high turnover — slippage and trading cost quietly erode the spread.
A headline yield well above peers — almost always extra CCC exposure, not selection skill.

mostImportant: How the fund allocates across sectors — an actively managed go-anywhere mandate that mixes investment-grade corporates, high yield, securitized, and EM debt, with the sleeve weights shifting at the manager's discretion rather than tracking a single index.
The character of the resulting portfolio — credit risk and yield come mostly from the high-yield and EM sleeves, so total return tracks credit spreads far more than the Treasury curve.
The nature of its income — a high distribution yield built from below-investment-grade and emerging-market coupons, paid monthly as ordinary income.
greenFlags: A through-cycle track record where the fund cut credit/EM exposure ahead of 2020 and 2022 drawdowns (PIMCO, Loomis Sayles) — evidence the go-anywhere mandate is used defensively, not just to harvest yield.
Distribution funded by portfolio yield rather than return of capital — a 19a-1 notice showing little or no ROC means the payout is earned, not your own principal returned.
Disclosed sleeve-level credit-quality and duration breakdowns updated monthly — transparency that lets you see the real risk behind a headline 6-7% yield.
redFlags: A distribution yield well above peers paired with a falling NAV over multiple years — the payout is being financed by return of capital, eroding principal.
Heavy use of derivatives, leverage, or CCC-and-below buckets to reach a yield target — magnifies the 2020-style drawdown the multisector label is supposed to soften.
Style drift into a near-permanent maximum high-yield/EM posture — you bought flexibility but own a static junk fund that sells off hard when spreads widen past ~500 bps.

mostImportant: What collateral and tranches it holds — a fund concentrated in structured credit (CLOs, ABS, non-agency RMBS, CMBS), where return depends on the seniority of the tranche and the credit of the underlying loan pool, not on a corporate issuer.

The character of the resulting portfolio — floating-rate CLO and ABS exposure carries little duration but real subordination risk, so losses come from collateral defaults eating through junior tranches in a credit downturn.
The nature of its income — a high floating-rate coupon that resets with short-term rates, paid as ordinary income and sensitive to where in the capital structure the fund sits.
greenFlags: A portfolio skewed to AAA/AA CLO tranches (JAAA, ICLO) — these sit above ~30-40% subordination and took near-zero principal losses even through 2008, so the yield is paid for liquidity not default risk.
Transparent disclosure of tranche-level attachment points and weighted-average collateral rating — lets you verify the fund isn't reaching for yield in BB/equity tranches.
Manager with a deal-sourcing and surveillance team across cycles (Janus Henderson, PGIM) — structured credit rewards underwriting depth that an index can't replicate.
redFlags: A concentration in BB or equity CLO tranches to lift yield — these are first-loss pieces that can be wiped out when collateral defaults spike, as 2020 mark-downs showed.
Wide bid-ask and gating risk in stress — structured credit can become untradeable, so the ETF traded at a steep discount to NAV in March 2020 even for high-quality holdings.
Opaque or stale collateral marks — when the fund can't show current attachment points and pool performance, a benign-looking NAV can hide deteriorating subordination.

mostImportant: What it holds and how it weights — mostly bank and insurance preferred and hybrid securities, often a rules-based index heavily concentrated in financial issuers because banks are the dominant issuers of preferreds.

The character of the resulting portfolio — these are deeply subordinated, long-duration or perpetual instruments that behave like a hybrid of long bonds and equity, so they fall in both rate spikes and credit stress.
The nature of its income — a high yield often paid as qualified dividends (for $25-par retail preferreds) rather than ordinary income, a meaningful after-tax advantage versus bonds.
greenFlags: A high share of qualified-dividend income (PFFV, PGX structure) — the after-tax yield can beat a higher-coupon bond fund for a taxable holder.
Diversification beyond US banks into insurance, utilities, and $1000-par institutional preferreds — reduces the single-sector blowup risk that hit pure bank-preferred funds in March 2023.
An index that screens out or limits non-cumulative and extension-risk structures — avoids the issues most likely to skip dividends or never get called.
redFlags: Near-total concentration in bank preferreds (PFF-style) — a banking-sector shock like March 2023 hit these securities far harder than the broad bond market.
Heavy weight in fixed-rate perpetual preferreds priced well above call — when rates rose they showed bond-like duration losses of 15%+, and when rates fell the best names got called away.
A large slug of non-cumulative preferreds from weaker issuers — dividends can be skipped without ever being repaid, unlike a missed bond coupon.

mostImportant: What it tracks and in which currency — hard-currency (mostly USD-denominated) sovereign and quasi-sovereign EM debt, typically an index like the JPM EMBI, so currency risk is limited but country credit risk is the main driver.

The character of the resulting portfolio — a long list of sovereign issuers spanning investment-grade (e.g. Mexico, Indonesia) down to distressed frontier names, where a single default (Argentina, Russia, Sri Lanka) can mark a position to a fraction of par.
The nature of its income — a high USD coupon that compensates for sovereign default and geopolitical risk, paid as ordinary income.
greenFlags: A credit-quality tilt toward higher-rated sovereigns with a capped weight per country (EMB, VWOB) — limits the damage when one frontier issuer defaults or gets sanctioned.
Exclusion or quick removal of sanctioned/defaulted issuers from the index — funds that held Russian debt to maturity in 2022 took avoidable marks.
Spread-per-unit-of-rating that's wide versus US high yield at comparable ratings — genuine compensation for EM risk rather than just duration.
redFlags: Outsized single-country exposure to a fiscally fragile sovereign — concentration that turns one restructuring into a fund-level loss, as Argentina and Lebanon showed.
A heavy frontier/CCC sleeve reaching for yield — these names gap down 30-50% on default with little recovery, unlike a diversified IG sovereign book.
A label that hides local-currency or corporate exposure — adds FX and issuer risk the buyer of a hard-currency sovereign fund didn't expect.

mostImportant: How it differs from a core bond fund — an unconstrained, benchmark-agnostic strategy that can go short duration, hold negative duration, use derivatives, and take long/short credit positions rather than track an aggregate index.
The character of the resulting portfolio — returns depend almost entirely on the manager's tactical calls on rates and credit, so outcomes diverge widely across funds carrying the same label.
The nature of its income — yield and total return are manufactured from a flexible mix of credit, carry, and derivative positions rather than a stable bond coupon.
greenFlags: A genuinely low or negative correlation to the Agg over a full cycle — proof the unconstrained mandate delivers diversification rather than just being a closet high-yield fund.
Transparent disclosure of net duration, gross/net credit exposure, and derivative notionals — lets you see the real risk behind a smooth-looking NAV.
A manager that demonstrably dialed down risk before 2020 and 2022 (e.g. short-duration positioning into the rate spike) — the flexibility was used to protect capital.
redFlags: A high distribution yield generated by selling volatility or running large derivative carry trades — hidden tail risk that detonates in a liquidity shock.
Embedded leverage pushing gross exposure well above 100% — amplifies losses when both rate and credit bets move against the fund at once.
A smooth NAV that masks illiquid private or structured holdings — the calm line breaks suddenly when those positions have to be marked in stress.

mostImportant: What it holds and where it sits in the capital structure — senior-secured floating-rate leveraged loans to below-investment-grade companies, ranking ahead of bonds and backed by collateral, typically tracking the Morningstar LSTA Loan Index.

The character of the resulting portfolio — the floating-rate coupon means almost no duration risk, so the entire risk is corporate default and recovery, and losses come from spread widening and defaults, not rising rates.
The nature of its income — a high coupon that resets with SOFR, so the distribution rises when short rates rise and falls when the Fed cuts, paid as ordinary income.
greenFlags: A higher weighted-average recovery and a low CCC bucket (BKLN, SRLN) — senior-secured loans historically recover ~60-70 cents versus ~40 for unsecured high-yield bonds, so quality collateral cushions defaults.
Disclosed exposure to second-lien and covenant-lite loans kept modest — these are the loans where 2020-style defaults produced thin recoveries.
An ETF that managed T+ settlement and liquidity well in March 2020 — bank loans settle slowly, and funds that didn't gate or trade at deep discounts proved their plumbing.
redFlags: A large CCC/second-lien sleeve reaching for yield — these default earliest and recover least, so the fund's downside in a credit cycle far exceeds its senior-secured label.
A persistent NAV discount and redemption stress in selloffs — loans are illiquid and slow-settling, so the ETF can trade well below NAV exactly when you want out (March 2020).
Heavy concentration in a few highly levered sponsors or sectors — turns idiosyncratic LBO defaults into a fund-level drawdown.

mostImportant: What it holds — below-investment-grade and unrated municipal bonds (tobacco, land-secured/dirt, healthcare, project finance), where credit analysis of the specific project matters more than a state's general-obligation rating.
The character of the resulting portfolio — long-duration and thinly traded, so it carries both real default risk on weak projects and sharp liquidity-driven price swings in stress.
The nature of its income — a high coupon that is federally tax-exempt, so the taxable-equivalent yield for a top-bracket investor is materially higher than the stated yield.
greenFlags: A taxable-equivalent yield that beats comparable-risk taxable high yield for a top-bracket holder (HYD, HYMB) — the tax exemption is the whole point and only pays off after-tax.
Diversification across many small issues with capped single-project weight — limits the damage from one Puerto Rico- or land-deal-style default.
Low exposure to the most default-prone sectors (speculative dirt bonds, distressed hospitals) — avoids the credits that drove past muni-HY blowups.
redFlags: Heavy concentration in tobacco settlement, land-secured, or a single distressed state (e.g. pre-restructuring Puerto Rico) — sector bets that turn one event into a fund-level loss.
A large unrated sleeve with little disclosed project-level analysis — you can't see the default risk you're being paid for.
Severe NAV discounts and redemption pressure in muni selloffs (March 2020, 2022) — the asset class is illiquid, so forced selling marks even sound bonds far below fair value.

mostImportant: What it tracks and the currency it pays in — EM sovereign debt denominated in local currencies (peso, real, rand), typically a GBI-EM-style index, so the dominant driver is FX moves against the dollar rather than credit spreads.
The character of the resulting portfolio — for an unhedged US investor, returns swing primarily with the dollar and EM currency volatility, with the local-rate and credit components secondary.
The nature of its income — high local-currency coupons reflecting EM inflation and policy rates, paid as ordinary income and exposed to currency translation.
greenFlags: Diversification across many currencies with per-country caps (EMLC, LEMB) — keeps a single currency crisis (Turkish lira, Argentine peso) from dominating returns.
A real-yield (nominal coupon minus local inflation) that is genuinely high — compensation for FX risk rather than yield that just offsets a depreciating currency.
A tilt toward countries with credible, independent central banks and positive real rates — currencies more likely to hold value through a dollar-strength cycle.
redFlags: Large weight in a high-inflation, weak-policy currency — the coupon gets eaten by depreciation, producing negative dollar returns despite a fat headline yield.
A label investors confuse with the hard-currency version — the FX exposure means a strong-dollar year can deliver double-digit losses with zero defaults.
Concentration in commodity-exporter currencies that move together — diversification on paper but a single correlated drawdown when commodities fall.

mostImportant: What it holds and how it behaves — bonds convertible into equity, giving an asymmetric profile that tracks the issuer's stock on the upside and acts like a credit-sensitive bond on the downside via the bond floor.
The character of the resulting portfolio — issuance is dominated by growth and tech companies, so the fund carries a strong equity-style tilt and rises and falls largely with that sector.
The nature of its income — a low coupon (the conversion option is paid for with yield), so most of the total return is expected to come from equity upside, not income.
greenFlags: A portfolio of balanced convertibles trading near parity (CWB, ICVT) — these capture the most equity upside while keeping a meaningful bond floor, the asymmetry the asset class is bought for.
Credit quality solid enough that the bond floor actually holds — the downside cushion only works if the issuer can repay, which is what failed for distressed names in 2008.
Diversification beyond mega-cap tech issuers — reduces the risk that the fund is just a leveraged bet on a single hot sector's equity.
redFlags: A heavy weight in deeply out-of-the-money 'busted' converts — these have lost their equity sensitivity and trade purely on weak credit, with little upside left.
Concentration in speculative-grade growth issuers (2021 SPAC/tech vintage) — when the stocks fell, the thin bond floor gave way and the converts dropped like equity.
Distributions padded by return of capital to mask the structurally low coupon — a payout that quietly erodes NAV.

mostImportant: What it holds and how it's valued — direct loans to private middle-market companies (often via BDC holdings or interval-fund structures), marked to model rather than to an observable market price.

The character of the resulting portfolio — illiquid, floating-rate senior loans whose true risk is sponsor and borrower default, with a smooth NAV that understates real volatility because the assets aren't traded daily.
The nature of its income — a very high floating-rate yield reflecting illiquidity and credit risk, paid as ordinary income.
greenFlags: Third-party or transparent valuation of holdings with disclosed mark methodology — reduces the appraisal-smoothing that lets weak books look stable.
Underlying BDCs trading near or below NAV with covered dividends and low non-accruals — evidence the high yield is earned, not borrowed against deteriorating loans.
A first-lien, senior-secured tilt with diversified sponsor exposure — the part of private credit with the best recovery when a borrower defaults.
redFlags: A rising non-accrual rate or growing PIK (payment-in-kind) income share in the underlying loans — borrowers paying with more debt instead of cash, a classic pre-default signal.
A suspiciously smooth NAV through 2022 stress — model marks hiding real credit deterioration that surfaces abruptly when loans are restructured.
Layered leverage or wide premiums to NAV on the underlying BDCs — you're paying up for, and amplifying, illiquid credit risk.

mostImportant: What it tracks — a diversified corporate-credit index spanning investment-grade and high-yield issuers, so return is driven by aggregate credit spreads across the quality spectrum rather than a single rating band.

The character of the resulting portfolio — it blends the modest spread and longer duration of IG with the higher default risk of HY, giving a middle-of-the-road credit beta that widens with the whole market in a risk-off move.
The nature of its income — a coupon between pure IG and pure HY, paid as ordinary income, reflecting the blended credit risk taken.
greenFlags: A transparent, rules-based quality and liquidity screen on the index — keeps the fund from drifting into the least-liquid CCC names to juice yield.
Tight tracking and effective in-kind creation/redemption in credit stress — IG/HY ETFs that held near NAV in March 2020 proved their basket liquidity.
A spread-per-unit-of-duration that compensates for the credit taken — yield earned from credit risk, not from quietly extending duration.
redFlags: A creeping shift down in quality (rising BB/CCC weight) without a name change — credit-beta drift that raises the 2020-style drawdown the buyer didn't sign up for.
Persistent NAV discounts and widening bid-ask in selloffs — a sign the underlying bonds are less liquid than the daily-traded ETF implies.
Heavy issuer or sector concentration (e.g. energy in a spread-widening shock) — turns a 'broad' label into a concentrated bet that gaps in a downturn.
